channel.title: math.HO updates on arXiv.org
channel.description: math.HO updates on the arXiv.org e-print archive.

item.title: Thomassen's proof and Filippov's proof of the Weak Jordan Theorem
item.description: arXiv:2604.04296v1 Announce Type: new Abstract: We present, in detail and with rigour, the two title proofs. The Weak Jordan Theorem states that the complement of any topological circuit in the plane is disconnected.
item.dc:creator: Martin Klazar

item.title: The illusion of illusions: There are no optical corrections in the Parthenon
item.description: arXiv:2510.16831v2 Announce Type: replace Abstract: One of the oldest and most enduring myths in human history is the belief that the Parthenon was cleverly designed with various curved structures and sizes in order to correct optical illusions, and therefore appear straight and regular. The myth has its origin in the writings of Vitruvius more than 2,000 years ago and was renewed in the nineteenth century when curved profiles were carefully measured. At least twelve different \textit{optical corrections} have been proposed, all following the same basic principle. The myth is still widely acknowledged as an obvious truth despite a complete absence of historical or scientific evidence. This paper analyzes these corrections scientifically and demonstrates that the illusions they are supposed to correct are either non-existent or so small as to be imperceptible.
item.dc:creator: Alain Goriely

item.title: Role of (periodic as well as aperiodic) tessellations in contemporary composition. The cases of Tesselles sonores and Le Chapeau \`a douze cornes by Marisa Acu\~na
item.description: arXiv:2601.15179v3 Announce Type: replace Abstract: The recent discovery of a family of aperiodic monotiles, which includes David Smith's famous Hat, has shaken the field of plane tessellations. Music composers have already utilised the visual representation of plane tilings in their artwork (Tom Johnson through his use of Vuza's canons, talea and color in isorhythmic motets...). Constructions of the Hat from elementary geometric polygons provide a different perspective, for example through the sound transformation of microtonal intervals, as seen in Marisa Acu\~na's piece Le Chapeau \`a douze cornes.
item.dc:creator: Maria Luisa Acu\~na Fuentes, \'Edouard Thomas

item.title: Mathematicians in the age of AI
item.description: arXiv:2603.03684v3 Announce Type: replace Abstract: Recent developments show that AI can prove research-level theorems in mathematics, both formally and informally. This essay urges mathematicians to stay up-to-date with the technology, to consider the ways it will disrupt mathematical practice, and to respond appropriately to the challenges and opportunities we now face.
item.dc:creator: Jeremy Avigad
